metadata.title: Playing to Save Sisters: How Female Gaming Communities Foster Social Support within Different Cultural Contexts

abstract:
Gender bias frequently exists in gaming culture, and online communities have formed separatist safe spaces to provide female players with social support. This study is based on text mining of female player communities on two platforms, Reddit and Douban. Text analysis methods containing Structural Topic Model and Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count were employed to explore how social support is presented and how topics and language features -including community identity, affection, and gender-related expressionsinfluence the social support that a post receives. Similarities and differences in these relationships are compared between two communities, representing Western individualist and Chinese collectivist cultures.

Should some spaces be for women only? A safe space is a concept frequently used in feminist discussions, which highlights that women cannot safely express themselves cognitively, emotionally, and intellectually unless they are free from harassment, abuse, misogyny, and other disturbing factors (Lewis et al., 2015). Separatism is a common method for constructing a feminist safe space: that is, marginalized groups with a shared identity create places of refuge, provide support for each other, and form collective resistance (The Roestone Collective, 2014). sections.header_raw: Girls' Resonance: Female Gaming Community in the Gaming Culture
sections.header_norm: girls resonance female gaming community in the gaming culture
paragraphs: Although the number of FGs is expanding annually, the gender imbalance within general gaming community exists persistently, and may originate from the "bastion of misogyny" embedded in gaming culture, "which is rooted at least partially in the presentation of gamers as male and gaming as a masculine sphere" (Cote, 2018, p. 496). Historically, digital games have been constructed as technologies for males (Cote, 2018), and the masculinized logic in games manifests itself in the following two ways.

First, the gendered content is included in game narratives, such as the overrepresentation of male characters versus the absence or sexualization of female characters (Lynch et al., 2016;Williams et al., 2009). In contrast to male characters who tend to have stronger combative powers, female characters tend to be supportive or rescued (Davies et al., 2020). According to a content analysis of 60 console games, Downs and Smith (2010) found that the proportion of male characters (86%) was much higher than female characters (14%), while 41% of the female characters wore revealing clothing or had abnormal body figures. The gender-bias content in games reinforces sexual stereotypes within the gamers' communities (Yao et al., 2022). Another gender logic is reflected in the gaming experience of FGs. Namely, a number of studies have indicated that females frequently encounter cyberbullying and discrimination when they access the gaming sphere as "intruders" (Chappetta & Barth, 2022;Kuznekoff & Rose, 2013). For instance, Kuznekoff and Rose (2013) used live recordings of game matchmaking and discovered that although there were no significant differences in victory rates between male and female players, feminine voices in game-based communication received three times as many negative comments as male voices or voiceless communication. Two masculinized logics also form vicious cycles: stereotypes exacerbate gender inequality in gaming, and discrimination may deter females from entering the games (Bergstrom, 2019;Fox & Tang, 2014), which further deepens the gender inequality in gaming communities. The dominant masculine gaming culture has rendered FGs subordinate to the gaming communities and has compressed the feminine voice in the gaming communities. Thus, most of the previous research cannot reasonably outline the characteristics of FGs since the majority of them remain silent within general game communities (Kivijärvi & Katila, 2022).

Given the disadvantaged position of girls within the gaming context, many FGs have chosen to form or join FGCs to raise their voices in a united manner. For example, PMS Clan, founded in 2002, is dedicated to creating a safe environment for FGs and is considered the most prominent FGC in the contemporary world (Richard & Hoadley, 2013). Richard (2017) indicated that communities such as PMS Clan help FGs build social networks and strengthen their confidence, which in turn encourages women's resistance to social restrictions and hierarchies. Another study uncovered that inter-membership practices of PMS Clan contribute to the accumulation of social capital, thereby building community resilience through interactions among members and challenging the male-dominated gaming culture (Richard & Gray, 2018). Besides, the resistance of the FGC may also contribute to demonstrating feminine power in the patriarchal society (Peña, 2013). Some other female-friendly gamer communities have also attracted academic attention. For example, some scholars have explored the status of women in eSports by examining text on FGCs on Reddit (Siutila & Havaste, 2018). In 2020, Douban, a Reddit-like platform in China, established a group called "Female Gamer Federation" (FGF), which rapidly became an influential forum for FGs by explicitly declaring in its rules that it only accepts gamers of female biological sex. One study collected all the texts of the group and revealed that the group members strengthened their in-group identity by rethinking the sexual images of game characters, resisting stereotypes, and building a cyber family through a self-constructed discourse system. FGF segregates patriarchal values through the self-constructed community and forms online social support among individuals through interactions such as posts and comments (Mu et al., 2022). Although previous studies have provided evidence of the protective and supportive environments that women-only "clans" offer, where harassment can be minimized (Kennedy, 2011), there is a lack of scholarly attention devoted to examining the distribution and factors that influence social support within these communities. This research gap is significant considering the pivotal role social support plays in safeguarding vulnerable populations, like FGs, within the gaming culture.

Certainly, both gaming culture and gender perceptions are largely conditioned and influenced by macro-society and culture, such as the collectivism upheld in East Asia versus the West-promoted individualism (Hofstede, 1984). Some scholars argue that Western and Asian gaming cultures are two gaming worlds developed on completely different values (Chen, 2014), which called for a transcultural perspective to observing FGCs. Western video games are always driven by technology and tend to highlight individual heroic narratives and power fantasies, with many shooting and strategy games enlisting the hardcore population. Hence, Western gaming culture is often seen as full of "militaristic masculinity" (Chen, 2014), which mirrors the absolute in-game dominance of masculine power. Conversely, Easterndesigned games are profoundly influenced by culture instead of technology (Chen, 2014), and guided by collectivism, the gaming cultures emphasize collective well-being and step-by-step grinding, yet the lengthy developmental process included in Asian games has also become a double-edged sword that may attract audiences yet kill the fun (Wayne, 2015). Additionally, based on the abundant anime resources in East Asia, "Moe" culture, which is represented by the sweet, pure, and innocent "Kawaii" esthetic, has developed a significant influence on the gaming industry, as FGs, who are neglected by Western games, seem to be more welcomed in East Asia (e.g. the first Otome Game Angelique was released in Japan in 1994; Chen, 2014;Kim, 2009). However, gendered risks are also implied in Moe culture: Yiu and Chan (2013) disclosed that Moe culture is based on a gaze that does not perceive the female body as a unified whole, but instead sees it as fragmented sexualized body parts. Tompkins et al. (2020) suggest that Moe culture echoes benevolent sexism, such as limiting the empowerment of females to the fantastical realm through producing cute magical girl images. Thus, East Asian gaming culture may not fundamentally differ from traditional masculine gaming culture regarding underlying gender perspectives. Although some studies have been conducted on FGs and FGCs in Chinese (e.g. Liu & Lai, 2022) and Western countries (e.g. Richard & Gray, 2018) respectively, given the impacts of the macro-cultural environment on gaming culture and gamers, a transcultural comparison is necessary and crucial for FGCs in different contexts.

Overall, there are three research gaps in previous studies of FGCs. First, most studies continue to focus on FGs within general gaming communities, rather than exclusive communities of FGs. These studies treat FGs as part of male-dominated gaming communities, yet male domination makes some FGs reluctant to acknowledge their gamer identification (Kuss et al., 2022), or FGs who are submerged in a culture of male hegemony opt to obey and be silent for membership (Kivijärvi & Katila, 2022), making it challenging for female voices to be truly present in the gaming community. Second, many studies have interpreted how FGs struggle against the external patriarchal culture from the perspective of confrontation, while fewer studies have focused on how mutual social support is generated by group members. Third, the studies on FGCs lack cross-cultural comparisons. However, considering the cultural diversity reflected in the Eastern and Western games, whether there are similarities and differences displayed in FGCs is also an essential issue to examine. Besides, when studying FGCs the contents are very important to be investigated because gaming community discourse accommodates members' gaming interpretation and identity (Perreault & Lynch, 2022). Accordingly, the current study adopts the GirlGamers on Reddit and the FGF forum on Douban as cases to depict and compare the linguistic features and social support of FGCs in Chinese and Western contexts respectively.

sections.header_raw: The Forgotten Corner: Female Gaming Communities and Social Support
sections.header_norm: the forgotten corner female gaming communities and social support
paragraphs: Social psychologists define social support as a system of resource exchange that allows people to feel cared for, loved, respected, and seen as part of a social network (Cobb, 1976;Sarason et al., 1986). Obtaining social support has also been proven to be positive for people's physical and mental health (Cohen et al., 2004). With the development of social media, social support has shifted online, empowering vulnerable groups to connect with like-minded individuals and voice their concerns anonymously (White & Dorman, 2001). Wright et al. (2011) classified online social support into three categories -emotional support, informational support, and (in some cases) instrumental support -and this classification has been validated across platforms and cultural contexts (e.g., Frohlich & Zmyslinski-Seelig, 2012; Shi & Chen, 2014). Within online communities, the simplest and most direct social support involves replying to others' posts. A previous study (Wang et al., 2017) pointed out that posters (the people who post) receive direct support from others' replies, and when other users engage in the discussion, the posters also receive social support by viewing these supportive messages. In other words, the more heatedly discussed posts in a community will yield more social support. Throughout previous research, the number of replies has often been used as an indicator of the strength of social support (Pan et al., 2017(Pan et al., , 2020)), while the length of replies can be used to measure the quality of supportive messages (Pan & Liu, 2021).

Text as the main content of an online community is helpful to understand what community members think and provide material for behavioral prediction (e.g., Jelodar & Frank, 2021). Varying discussion topics on online forums may also have an impact on the social support that a post can receive. For example, self-disclosure has been found effective in eliciting emotional support, whereas question-asking is related to informational support (Wang et al., 2015). On this basis, this study posed the following research questions:
RQ1: What topics are discussed in the FGCs on Reddit and Douban respectively?
RQ2: How do these topics affect the social support users receive?

Apart from the extracted textual themes, the linguistic features, as a window exhibiting users' cognitive and psychological processes, also affect social support (Pan & Liu, 2021;Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). The use of firstperson plural pronouns such as "we" can reflect a sense of collective identification (Brewer & Gardner, 1996;Gustafsson Sendén et al., 2014), which also inspired us to investigate the relationship between social support and the expression of identification in FGCs. Also, the expression of emotional states has always been an important variable influencing social support and even subtle emotional arousal can reshape people's subsequent behaviors (Nabi, 2003). For instance, positive emotions have been found to be the most frequently expressed emotion in online supportive groups (Klemm & Wheeler, 2005). What's more, owing to the prominence of gender issues in FGCs (Mu et al., 2022;Richard & Gray, 2018), we also need to ascertain whether the use of gender-related words in posts affects the offering and receiving of social support. In summary, we proposed the following question: Do posting texts with collective identification (RQ3), affection (RQ4), and gender-related expressions (RQ5) affect the social support received by FGs on Reddit and Douban?

Throughout previous research, the number of replies has often been used as an indicator of the strength of social support (Pan et al., 2017(Pan et al., , 2020)), while the length of replies can be used to measure the quality of supportive messages (Pan & Liu, 2021).

Text as the main content of an online community is helpful to understand what community members think and provide material for behavioral prediction (e.g., Jelodar & Frank, 2021). Varying discussion topics on online forums may also have an impact on the social support that a post can receive. For example, self-disclosure has been found effective in eliciting emotional support, whereas question-asking is related to informational support (Wang et al., 2015). On this basis, this study posed the following research questions:

RQ1: What topics are discussed in the FGCs on Reddit and Douban respectively?

RQ2: How do these topics affect the social support users receive?

Apart from the extracted textual themes, the linguistic features, as a window exhibiting users' cognitive and psychological processes, also affect social support (Pan & Liu, 2021;Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). The use of firstperson plural pronouns such as "we" can reflect a sense of collective identification (Brewer & Gardner, 1996;Gustafsson Sendén et al., 2014), which also inspired us to investigate the relationship between social support and the expression of identification in FGCs. Also, the expression of emotional states has always been an important variable influencing social support and even subtle emotional arousal can reshape people's subsequent behaviors (Nabi, 2003). For instance, positive emotions have been found to be the most frequently expressed emotion in online supportive groups (Klemm & Wheeler, 2005). What's more, owing to the prominence of gender issues in FGCs (Mu et al., 2022;Richard & Gray, 2018), we also need to ascertain whether the use of gender-related words in posts affects the offering and receiving of social support. In summary, we proposed the following question: Do posting texts with collective identification (RQ3), affection (RQ4), and gender-related expressions (RQ5) affect the social support received by FGs on Reddit and Douban?

Obtaining social support has also been proven to be positive for people's physical and mental health (Cohen et al., 2004). With the development of social media, social support has shifted online, empowering vulnerable groups to connect with like-minded individuals and voice their concerns anonymously (White & Dorman, 2001).

paragraphs: The current study focused on FGCs, selected GirlGamers on Reddit and FGF on Douban as two typical cases, and employed text mining to find out the similarities and differences in the social support of FGCs across different cultural contexts. Douban and Reddit are popular interest group platforms in China and English-speaking countries respectively, with similar online forum interface designs and same affordance for users to form groups. GirlGamers and FGF are the largest gatherings of FGs on Reddit and Douban, with the former having nearly 190,000 members and the latter having over 40,000 users. Given the representation of the platforms and the size of the user base, the present study can better delve into the community expression and social support of FGs under East -West cultural contexts.

paragraphs: Posts from GirlGamers on Reddit and FGF on Douban were collected respectively through a self-written Python program by one of the authors. The collection period was from October 28 to December 28, 2021, and the collected entries included the poster's username, title, posting time, and content, as well as the time and content of replies. Sample of our data is presented in Supplementary Material A. Eventually, the current study collected 7,385 posts from 6,193 users on GirlGamers (which contained 24,258 replies) and 1,748 posts from 1,647 users on FGF (which contained a total of 14,296 replies). The ratio of the number of posted users (.27) is close to the ratio of the total number of users in the two communities (.23).

paragraphs: For answering RQ1 and RQ2, STM, as an unsupervised machine learning approach, can reveal hidden distinctions and associations between texts while linking textual topics to metadata by introducing covariates (Maier et al., 2018), so as to uncover the topics of the gaming community while further verifying the statistically significant associations between topics and other data indicators. STM treats each text as an aggregation of multiple topics based on the prior distribution, estimates potential topics based on this premise, and also combines external covariates with the topic's prior distribution (Chen et al., 2020). Compared to other topic modeling approaches, STM affords the ability to combine the latent content and contextual information, thus offering a comprehension toward textual context (Fresneda et al., 2021). STM can output the words with the highest exclusive weight under each topic and then assist researchers in judging the connotation of the topic (Roberts et al., 2019). The stm package in R was utilized to build structural topic models.

paragraphs: To interpret RQ3-RQ5, hierarchical OLS regression was adopted, and automated language-processing software LIWC-2022 (Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count) was chosen to extract the textual linguistic feature for measurement. LIWC is a natural language processor that facilitates discovering the count and proportion of different word types (e.g., personal pronouns, auxiliaries, and prepositions) and then captures the underlying psychosocial processes (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). The original LIWC-22 dictionary (Boyd et al., 2022) was used for the English analysis, while the simplified Chinese LIWC dictionary compiled by Huang et al. (2012) was adopted for analyzing Douban's data. The same criteria were constructed for both languages, and the results of the two dictionaries show a high degree of consistency by examining more than 100 bilinguistic articles (Huang et al., 2012). The Chinese word separation was done by jieba package in R. After operationalizing the required variables through LIWC, this study used hierarchical OLS regression with SPSS 25.0 to clarify the relationships between the variables and to respond to the above hypotheses.
Language Features. Variables related to language features we extracted were introduced as follows.

Collective Identification. The use of first-person plural pronouns refers to the occurrence of words such as "we" and "our" in the text. A series of previous studies have explored the collective identity reflected in the firstperson subordinate pronouns (Brewer & Gardner, 1996;Gustafsson Sendén et al., 2014), which has been reconfirmed by the LIWC analysis (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). Affiliational words including "we," "help," and other words related to social connections have also been proven to be significantly related to the strength of collective identification (Ashokkumar & Pennebaker, 2022).

Affection. Positive emotion includes words such as "love" and "hope," whereas negative emotion contains words such as "anxiety" and "hate." To some extent, using affective words reflects the emotional state of the poster at that moment, which has also been repeatedly confirmed in different social media contexts (Bae & Lee, 2012;Sun et al., 2020).

Gender-Related Expression. The LIWC contains three gender-related phrases: sexual words, female references, and male references. Sexual words include words such as "sex" and "pregnant," which have been found to depict the gazes toward the female body (Khalifa & Essam, 2022). The female references include "girl" and "sister," while the male references correspondingly include "man," "brother," and other male referents. Previous studies have indicated that the level of textual sexualization can be ascertained by the number of gender-related words contained in the text (Gupta & Jordan, 2022;Park et al., 2022).

Social Support. The current study borrows Pan and Liu's (2021) definition of social support, employed in a study of online support forums, and measures social support by the length and number of user replies. The length of user replies indicates the quality of users' participation in online social support (Barak & Gluck-Ofri, 2007); the number of user replies means how many replies a poster receives from other users and is presented as the intensity of social support (Pan et al., 2017).
In addition, the length of the main post was included as a control variable for its influence on replies in the current model. See Supplementary Material B for detailed descriptive statistics.

sections.header_raw: Topics and Social Support in Female Gaming Communities
sections.header_norm: topics and social support in female gaming communities
paragraphs: As an unsupervised modeling method, STM requires researchers to determine the number of topics before modeling. After considering multiple-topic model judgment indicators such as held-out likelihood, residuals, and semantic consistency, we extracted six topics from Reddit text and generated four topics from Douban text to answer RQ1. The topics and corresponding keywords are shown in Table 1.

Topics about game information and recommendation of game products were classified as informational support, while discussions related to women's issues embodied emotional support. In the GirlGamers group, there were more topics closely related to games, such as recommendations for game products and external equipment. However, the proportion of comments from a gendered critical perspective was not high, and the gendered topics were still concentrated in the game space, such as role-setting in the game, as well as harassment and bullying that players encounter during the game experience. However, for FGF in Douban, although game recommendations also ranked first, more discussions focused on female identities. Here, FGs not only paid attention to female narratives and role settings; they also jumped out of the game context and shared gendered issues in real life, which was even the second-highest discussed topic. On the whole, informational support occupied a higher proportion than emotional support in the two communities.

In order to answer RQ2, this study also incorporated social support variables (namely, the length and number of user replies) into the model as covariates to test the relationship between social support and topics. Results are shown in Figures 1 and 2. In GirlGamers, the topic of female narratives in games led to longer user replies, while the topic of external gaming devices was positively associated with the number of replies. However, in FGF, the topic of women's status in reality was likely to receive longer replies, while replies to the topic of game product recommendations and female narratives in games were relatively shorter. In terms of the number of replies, the topic of female bodies and male gaze in games tended to spark widespread discussion.

paragraphs: To address RQ3-5, this study paid attention to the relationship between language features and social support in two FGCs. Based on LIWC, we obtained the vocabulary details in the main posts, and OLS regression was employed to determine the relationship we were concerned with. Results are shown in Table 2. For GirlGamers in Reddit, sexual words (β = .10, p < .01), female references (β = .10, p < .01), and male references (β = .12, p < .01) showed a positive effect on the length of replies. In contrast, only negative sentiment words (β = .08, p < .05) could attract a higher number of responses.

For FGF in Douban, affiliational words (β = .15, p < .01) and female words (β = .13, p < .01) both positively predicted the length of replies, while positive emotional words showed a negative correlation with the length of replies (β = -.10, p < .05). sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2023 - Playing to Save Sisters How Female Gaming Communities Foster Social Support within Different Cultur
paragraphs: By mining the texts of GirlGamers on Reddit and FGF on Douban, this study explored how the two FGCs establish social support in Eastern and Western cultural contexts respectively. Firstly, our findings revealed that each FGC formed and consolidated a supportive atmosphere in the community by superimposing game informational support and female emotional support.

Both communities had topics related to informational support ranking first, bridging the gaming information gap brought about by gender, which echoed a former study's findings that the lack of access to gaming information drove women out of the gaming community (McLean & Griffiths, 2019).
Behind the informational support was a female emotional context in which both FGCs offered a gendered filter to the discussions around games, and gendered topics formed the emotional support.

Secondly, the relationship between topics and social support fosters an understanding of similarities and differences between the two communities. FGs are in the intersectional power structure constructed by gender and gaming culture, and the two communities demonstrated different focuses on FGs' identity. In GirlGamers, a higher intensity of social support (i.e., more replies) was given to discussion of game devices, whereas the topic of the female body and the male gaze received a higher intensity of social support in FGF. This indicates that although both groups comprise FGCs, members in GirlGamers tend to identify themselves as gamers, while FGF has a stronger gender identity. To a certain extent, this difference between the two communities can be attributed to timing. GirlGamers started in 2010, and FGF started in China 10 years later; in the very early stage, gendered issues accounted for the highest proportion of discussions in FGF (Mu et al., 2022), while nowadays posts related to games have ranked first among all topics. The development of two communities suggests that the identity of players would be strengthened and gendered undertones retained. Despite this difference, a higher quality of social support (i.e., longer replies) was received for gender topics in both groups, which shows the shared gendered value of two FGCs.

Thirdly, different relationships involving language features and social support between GirlGamers and FGF signal the influence of culture on the FGCs. Hofstede (1984) used individualism/collectivism to describe possible forms of relationships between individuals and the groups to which they belong, and the distinction is distinguishable in individualism while blurred in collectivism (Bochner, 1994). Language use can transmit cultural differences (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010), and compared with the English context, there is a deep-rooted collectivism in Chinese culture (Chung & Mallery, 1999), emphasizing the interdependence between individuals and others. For the language feature relating to collective identity, neither first-person plural pronouns nor affiliational words influenced social support in GirlGamers, while these respective aspects positively predicted the number and length of replies in FGF. People who believe in collectivism are more inclined to communicate with community members to achieve collective goals, so in this case, using words related to social identity in conversation could evoke a sense of community and generate social support, while this outcome was not as pronounced in individualist contexts. In terms of the language feature of affection, two communities showed the same effect of negative emotional words on the number of replies, while posts with positive emotional words triggered shorter replies in FGF but not in GirlGamers. We traced the texts containing emotional words in the two communities and found that positive emotional words often conveyed joy on an individual level (e.g., game clearance), while negative emotional words expressed anger on a collective level (e.g., women's experiences of being bullied in games). The description of collective phenomena received broad responses in both communities, which indicated that they shared the common goal to fight against external pressure and pursue female power in gaming culture. As for the negative relationship between positive emotional words and length of replies in FGF, an individual's joy about a specific game or situation would probably only attract players with the same gaming experience, rather than high-quality informational social support in the context of collectivist culture. Finally, the use of gender-related expressions all influenced social support in two communities to varying degrees. In GirlGamers, no matter what gender-related expressions were employed, they could promote repliers to publish long speeches as high-quality social support. However, in FGF, only female references could generate similar responses, which echoes the result in the relationship between topics and social support: discussions around women's status often brought out detailed resonance, while posts related to men generally involved masculinity and resistance in which more users wanted to engage.

We further ascribe the difference between two FGCs to the discrepancies in the background of broader Chinese and Western gaming cultures. Compared with GirlGamers, gendered-related topics in FGF occupy higher prevalence and are more specific and detailed, reflecting a more difficult gendered situation for FGs in the Chinese context. For instance, the topic of female body and male gaze in games only occurs in FGF and relates to a higher number of replies. This is not unexpected when considering that Moe culture, the typical gaming culture within East Asia, sees the female body as fragmented sexualized female body parts rather than an organic whole (Yiu & Chan, 2013), and also works effectively even in the context of the so-called "female gaze." "Female gaze" is believed as a resistance along with the increasing number of FGs and rising Otome games in the Chinese gaming industry, but in practice, censorship still aims at uncovered bodies of female avatars rather than male bodies, the true sexual material attracting the FGs to play Otome games (Lai & Liu, 2023), leaving Chinese FGs suppressed under powerful masculine culture. Otome games also perpetuate benevolent sexism through segregating FGs and limiting their opportunities to engage with the broader gaming culture. In contrast, intense gameplay is more popular among GirlGamers, which implies a comparatively more gender-inclusive atmosphere within Western gaming culture, suggesting that FGs to some extent have transcended gender boundaries and better engaged in the gaming realm. The trend is closely tied to the long-standing struggle history of FGs in the Western world. From the hasty ending of the Girl Game Movement in the 1990s to the resurgence of discussions regarding the status of FGs during the Gamergate controversy a decade ago, the fight for gender equality within gaming culture has been an enduring, well-established, and progressive one. In Western gaming culture, the FGrelated issue is not only a concern within the minorities but has evolved into a socially significant discourse through various movements, thereby reinstating the gaming characteristics in FGCs.

The FGC, like other feminist safe spaces, offers a secure environment for FGs within the patriarchal context. However, the nature of public online forums cannot guarantee absolute safety, leaving FGs vulnerable to the male gaze of game culture. While the FGC embraces diverse preferences and female discourses, it excludes male players and reinforces binary gender norms through membership and community boundaries. Social support is provided within the FGC, but its availability is limited by group dynamics and language style, resulting in the exclusion of certain topics. As Clark-Parsons (2018) aptly summarizes, the feminist safe space is "safe but unsafe, inclusive but exclusive, open to but limiting of discourse" (p. 2141). Thus, "safety" is not the sole defining characteristic of the FGC but a dynamic concept shaped by the social support it provides. While recognizing the empowering aspects of the FGC, it is crucial to critically examine its internal structure and external boundaries to better understand this phenomenon within the contemporary game culture.

FGC and the concept of social support provide a perspective with multiple possibilities for gendered game studies: the FGC is born in the virtual world but cannot be isolated from real life. Also, the community can resist dual pressures from the virtual world and reality for FGs and build daily social support. On the one hand, this kind of social support has to reflect the exclusivity in the cultural power structure in which it exists due to the limitations of real life (Mu et al., 2022); on the other hand, because of the digital practical characteristics of the virtual world, it retains the potential to surpass the old world and imagine a new world. Games are neither spiritual opiates nor neo-utopias, women are neither second-class citizens nor movement pioneers, and social support from the community is neither useless nor a panacea. It is through the tension between virtuality and reality, discipline and emancipation, support and repulsion that the FGC will become a promising topic in the intersection of game and gender studies.

Certainly, there are some limitations in this study. Firstly, the differences between Douban and Reddit are not limited to cultural contexts; the discrepancies of platform affordance also affect user behavior; secondly, the data we obtained on Douban has survived censorship, which could cause selection bias in sampling; thirdly, regarding the measurement on social support, this study employs LIWC for assistance, and although such operationalization has been certified in numerous studies of online forums, the approach still fails to distinguish the contents of the texts. As the very exploratory work in FGC-related research, we suggest forthcoming studies pay more attention to text content and apply some supervised machine learning approaches to assist in classification and measurement.
